speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Moses Kaufman. This is tape eight. Moses, we were talking about your trip towards Palestine. After you went through Czechoslovakia, where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Czechoslovakia, that was organized, actually, through the Brihah. We went to Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly, from Czechoslovakia to Vienna, by buses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you pay for the journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't pay. That was everything organized through the Brihah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were the buses not government buses? They were private?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't tell you. I-- I am positive-- sure that it was organized through the Brihah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, where did you end up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I end up in Vienna. [SIGHS] And that was Rothschild Hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the-- the all-- people what they suppose-- that was a transit, I would-- I would say. It's a transit place for the all people what were trying to go to-- to-- to-- to Palestine at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there only a few days-- two or three days. And we find out that everyone what is illegally coming to Palestine-- mandatorium of the British-- everyone illegally is going-- instead, they're going to a-- to a camp in Cyprus. My son was just a newborn baby, and I couldn't risk to go with a newborn baby-- to go and to be-- to go to-- you know, to be caught and to be-- so I decided to wait and to see. That according to me, it was horrible that people what went through that Gehenna. A Gehenna-- that is not a comparison with the whole history of the Jewish history that something like this could happen to people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was waiting till that will change. At that time in-- the government was carried on through-- what's that minister was? Bevin. He-- he was the one who was-- that everyone-- Jew, every Jew what is coming to Palestine, if he is illegally coming to Palestine, has to be, has to go to the-- to a camp-- not a concentration-- to a camp in Cyprus. So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was waiting for better times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you left the hospital. So where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have-- went to a hospital, and I-- and I-- and I-- and I-- I was in-- privately, I was renting a place there-- 1/2 a plat, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the number?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: With me was my cousin, Izoidor Zilbrszpatz, who came from Russia-- was with me in Danzig all the time. And when I left Danzig illegally, he was with me all the time through-- no-- through Czechoslovakia and Vienna. We were all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was-- Izoidor Zilbrszpatz, Ala, your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ala--

speaker: interviewer
text: --your son, Ian?

speaker: interviewee
text: --my-- my newborn son, yes, and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: A year.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in that year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That year-- waiting till everything will get better, that I will be able to go to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --to-- to-- to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't working.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you maintain your existence?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got still some money what left me over from the times when the war was going on, and money for what I was getting for my work when Poland was liberated. And I was putting in motion-- putting in motion. So that was the time. That was helping me out a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you associate with Jewish people while you lived in Vienna for that year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much, no. Mine idea, and everything-- what I want is only to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe that year in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna? Very hard. Everything was very costly-- very, very costly. Because to eat enough, and to have what I need for a child, and my wife and-- so it was really very costly. It was very costly.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was very costly, because everything was controllized through little coupons-- for bread, for-- for-- for-- you know. And that wasn't enough, you know? And so I was trying to buy. And I was buying all this through-- privately, you know? And that costs more money.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, that was shortly before a year. That was about 9 months being there, or 9 or 10 months. A family to my dear wife, they used to live in-- in Australia. They find out that we are alive. And they sent us the-- sent us in payment for me, for my wife, for my son, and for-- for-- for-- for-- for my cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what was the name of the family here in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the family was Liechtenstein. It was a brother. This was a uncle-- was an uncle to my wife-- a brother to his-- to her father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year did you come out to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in October, 1947. I came to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that directly from Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- through France. We were staying in France for a while-- not a short while. And through Marseille, 10 weeks on the waters in a-- in a-- in a ship, we came here. The ship called Sagittaire.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the ports--

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a lot to tell about that voyage-- 10 weeks on the waters to Australia from--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the-- what were the conditions like on the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hard to describe them too. But still, we were free. We were lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel lucky?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lucky, and not lucky. We were lucky if the way was to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What ports did you stop in on the way to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Marseille, Algeria, Algeria to Papeete Noumea, through-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --what do you call this? [NON-ENGLISH]. And which city did you land in when you arrived in Australia in October?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And from Sydney-- no, no, no, no. I-- we didn't stay in Sydney. From Sydney, by trains, we came to-- to-- to Melbourne.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who helped you establish yourself here in Melbourne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any Jewish communities--

speaker: interviewee
text: I thinks--

speaker: interviewer
text: --here?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I thinks-- I thinks my-- I thinks my special-- as a specialist, I established myself. After two months being here working for a place--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --what to do, how to do. I got a little tannery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bond Street, Davis Port.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was two months after arriving in the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was two-- no-- a little bit more than two months. It was nearly three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak the language? Did you speak English?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all-- nothing. What, English? No, nothing-- nothing at all-- not a word.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you communicate?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hard. Really, it was very hard. It was very hard. I was reading the papers, listening and that so-- and that was the way as I learned your language.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family, the Liechtensteins, help you out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They help me out that-- that-- no, I wouldn't say. No. I was-- I establish myself. No. I establish myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your main employment-- work-- since you arrived in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, I was looking something to do, and looking for something in my trade to do. And it tooks a long time until I find something-- a little tannery-- what used to do some rugs.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came here. I got not-- the only one thing what I got was 1,000 pound. So $750-- 750 pounds at that time I paid for the key money to be able to rent the place-- rent the place. Where did you get the 1,000 pounds from?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 1,000 pound I got from the-- from Johannesburg. When they find out where I-- who I am and what I was doing, and they got the whole correspondence I sent out to the grand rabbi, to Johannesburg, he sent me 1,000 pounds. And with that 1,000 pounds, I start.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just explain the connection between the rabbi in Johannesburg and why he was giving you that money?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was reading the letters written to him-- copies of letters-- written to him through his son-in-law, yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rabbi Lvovicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was the rabbi that you helped with financial assistance--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct

speaker: interviewee
text: Establishing myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in-- in Vilna.

speaker: interviewee
text: In getting-- renting a place for which I paid $750. And $250, that was the money I start a business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that the-- your-- your only--

speaker: interviewee
text: The only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: --employment?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only one money that I got.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you maintained that company until your retirement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till my retirement-- that's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here in Australia, did you establish a Jewish life for yourself and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. First of all, as a [NON-ENGLISH], I was one of the grandest-- no-- establishment of that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Founding?

speaker: interviewee
text: --society-- foundings-- founders of that establishment, of [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was working up to now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how else are you involved with the Jewish-- how else are you involved now with the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's from 1947, from 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you involved with any other work with Holocaust organizations, or Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish?

speaker: interviewer
text: --community groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, [NON-ENGLISH] the Hebrew University in Jerusalem and [NON-ENGLISH]. I'm still a member, and I am still working with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: To finish off, what message would you like to leave for people who'll be watching this testimony in years to come about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I would like to tell you that I have still to tell you, and what I already missed, would take hours, and hours, and hours, and hours. But the idea, that's a beautiful idea, I'm doing this for my children, for my grandchildren, for the generations what they will come in the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many grandchildren do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got two--

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: --beautiful, lovely grandchildren-- two granddaughters. And I am very proud--

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to having a son like this, and having-- one is Melanie-- the name, the older one. The younger one is Romi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's Ian's wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ian's wife's name is Gila. And he is also a daughter of parents from Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying about the message you'd like to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: The message is like this. Whatever will-- would said about the Holocaust, whatever has been written, whatever-- it's nothing in comparison to what really people could tell-- how that Gehenna, how they murder us, annihilate six million of Jews. I'm doing this for the generations what will come to remember this-- never to forget-- and to know exactly how they have to react. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The whole world knows about us, what happens to us. They didn't-- done nothing. They know. Churchill know, Roosevelt know. Now likely what I read in the paper-- the securities, just because that is trying to cover this. They didn't know about this, they didn't know about this. And I said then, the whole world know exactly what was going on about the Holocaust. And they did nothing. Likely in the papers, I read that the securities, in 1941-- they got the court, defying the court, the Germans what the general-- the generals sent from the front, from the east front to thems-- to-- to-- to the-- to the headquarters in Berlin, and so on, mentioning about how they are killing thousands and thousands of Jews. So they know about this, but didn't-- done nothing-- didn't done nothing to stop this. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So the testimonies-- actually, what the-- what left after that horrible times, you know-- what is no comparison with no other things what happens to the Jews-- take the old times, times from the Inquisition, times from-- from-- from [NON-ENGLISH] times from the old pogroms and so on-- no comparison-- no comparison. Let the people know, and let the generations know how they have not to forget this, and how they have to behave, and how they-- how they have to react.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Moses, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's my son, my dear son. No introduction, because he looks like me, I believe, yeah? I am his father. Is that correct? I'm very proud of you. I am more than proud of you. You are a one of a million. And I am thankful to God that I got a son like you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks, dad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ian, did your father speak to you as you were growing up about his experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were little snippets of information that came out in the early stages, but very, very little-- very, very brief; and certainly, I think, censored-- probably on purpose. I think as-- like a lot of other, sort of, Holocaust survivors, dad tried to protect me from a lot of things. He wanted things to go sweetly in life for me. And I think horrific memories and horrific stories of what he endured, and many others like him endured-- I think he protected me from those. There was a-- an episode in relatively recent years, which was precipitated by a particular event that resulted in, almost a catharsis, when, in the space of one evening, and this evening went till something like 5:00 in the morning, my father opened up. And it was as if there was this dam that had broken. And all-- I shouldn't say all-- but many of his experiences and his emotions flowed. And since that time, he has, on numerous occasions, related various incidents, various experiences. Usually at times when there is a family gathering, or a family tragedy, or illness, it seems to precipitate a-- an outpouring of words, and thoughts, and ideas, and feelings, and experiences. But it was only in recent years that this began.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is there anything you'd like to say to or about your father before we finish off?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very proud of my father. I'm very proud of his attitude, his strong belief, and his actions during what was a horrific time for him, and for many others like him. And I'm proud to be his son.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you to both of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. That's my father. This was when he was younger. He was most probably-- what-- 22, 24 years old. And the picture was taken in Radom. And I find the picture being once, years ago, in Israel, by my auntie and my uncle, Mr. Ginsberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother. That picture most probably was be taken when she was about 20, 22 years old. It was taken in Radom. And that picture, I got this out also being in Israel from my uncle and my auntie-- Ginsbergs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my father, my brother, late brother, and me, taken most probably about 75 or 78 years ago, also in Radom. That picture, that photograph, I got this also in Israel when I was visiting my auntie--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Mrs. Ginsberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name is Reuben.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one is he? Is he on the left, or the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's the-- on the left. I am the-- the older one, that is Reuben. I am the youngest one--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the little one, with the long hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is me when I was a student in gymnasium. That is, I believe, the fourth class, in gymnasium, in Radom, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time I was about 13 years, 14 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get a hold of the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a picture that I got this-- this, I think-- I believe that is from my auntie, Mrs. Ginsberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my dear sister, large sister. So after war, after 2 and a 1/2 years of being in concentration camp in Auschwitz as a political prisoner, I find her when the war still was going on. And I brought her to Lublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is the daughter of my sister, perished in 1943, in the Radom, with my auntie-- took her there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Gila Giza.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo was taken in-- just before the war in Krynica. That was a resort in Poland, Krynica.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did-- how did you get hold of the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo was given to me through one of my friends what got them to-- from Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you

speaker: interviewee
text: That is me, and that picture was taken in-- straightaway after the war when I was liberated, in Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the family Riszfeld, with Mr. Riszik Rischfeld, what-- who is on the left side, with whom I have been by the partisans. The little boy was the son, and he was in Vilna. And through a Polish family, the commandant of the underground, the Polish underground, was hiding him there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the one-- Jurek. His name was Jurek. And that was the wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: They both emigrated straightaway to America, where the parents are not anymore alive. But he, Urich, is still in America--

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: --lives in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lutka-- Lutka. And she was together with my dear wife during the war, hiding on the Aryan side.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they escape from the camp-- from Kailis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That photo was taken in 1948, in America already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: On that photo-- I wanna call her artist and my daughter too-- Gila-- that she's in the middle; and on the left side, the daughter, my granddaughter, Melanie; and on the right side, my granddaughter, dear Romi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a copy of letters I got from people that I helped during the war-- financially.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do the letters say?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the help what I was giving to them-- is written in Jewish, and in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who wrote this particular letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: This particular letter was written by the director of the Rosh Yeshiva Rabbi Lvovicz, in Vilna, whom I helped financially.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- I got this out from Jerusalem, from when my father was written in for his work he done in Radom, for the Zionist movement-- for 35 years work in Radom for the Zionist movement.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
